title: AI-Driven SERS for Non-invasive and Label-Free Extracellular Vesicle Detection Across Cellular Origins in Tears and Sweat
authors: Yang Li, Xiaoming Lyu, Ling Xia, Kuo Zhan, Haoyu Ji, Lei Qin, Seppo J. Vainio, Jian-An Huang
abstract: Wearable sensing technology capable of point-of-care, continuous and non-invasive analysis of exosomes in biofluid such as tears and sweat is an essential part for future personalized medicine. Major detection and identification methods of cell secreted Extracellular Vesicles (EVs) often require labeling and are time-consuming, resulting in low efficiency in EV mechanism research and disease diagnosis. While the label-free Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) has been combined with deep learning model for EV identification in blood, their application to non-invasive detection of EVs in tears and sweat are missing. Here, we filled this gap by developing an artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) method based on salt-induced nanoparticle aggregation for fast EV identification in tears and sweat with high accuracy. Significantly, our label-free detection and AI differentiation of EVs from 6 cell lines (HepG2, Hela, 143B, LO-2, BMSC, H8) achieved the identification of EVs in tear fluids from 7 different disease sources with accuracies >92%. Our results showed that this platform can not only distinguish EVs from multiple cell sources but also generate highly reproducible and selective EV signals in tear fluids without a need for chemical labeling or separation steps. Molecular dynamics simulations revealed that silver atoms (Ag) form electrostatic interactions with oxygen atoms of multiple amino acid residues in proteins, suggesting a high affinity. This strategy realizes ultra-sensitive and anti-interference detection of EVs, providing a new idea for the rapid diagnosis of clinical diseases.
summary: This paper presents an AI-driven SERS-based sensing platform for the label-free detection of extracellular vesicles in tears and sweat. By combining nanoparticle-induced Raman enhancement with deep learning, the researchers achieved high-accuracy identification of disease-specific vesicles. This approach offers a potential path toward non-invasive, point-of-care diagnostics for various clinical conditions.
main_problem: The need for a non-invasive, label-free, and rapid method to detect and identify Extracellular Vesicles (EVs) from various cellular origins in biofluids like tears and sweat.
main_result: Developed an AI-assisted SERS platform that achieves over 92% accuracy in identifying EVs from 7 different disease sources in tears and successfully differentiates EVs from 6 different cell lines.
method: The method uses salt-induced aggregation of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) to enhance Raman signals, combined with machine learning models including PCA-SVM for cell origin classification and CNN/RNN for disease identification.
model_or_system: An experimental platform consisting of silver nanoparticles (Ag@BO) interacting with EVs in biological fluids (tears and sweat), supported by Molecular Dynamics simulations of protein-AgNP electrostatic interactions.
key_observables: Raman spectral fingerprints (specifically peaks at 845, 997, and 1654 cm-1), EV size (100-150 nm), and classification accuracy/confusion matrices.
important_parameters: Sample volume of 10 uL, 532 nm laser wavelength, and 70/30 training/testing data split.
main_assumptions: Assumes that the molecular signatures in EVs accurately reflect the pathophysiological status of the parental cells and that the SERS signal is representative of the EV composition.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Workflow of EV detection and ML pipeline; Fig 2: EV isolation, TEM, and Western Blot validation; Fig 4: SERS profiles and PCA/HCA clustering; Fig 6: 3D PCA and sweat analysis; Fig 7: Tear collection workflow and ROC curves for CNN/RNN/SVM.
paper_structure: Introduction of the clinical need -> Experimental platform development (AgNP synthesis and EV isolation) -> Characterization of EVs (TEM, NTA, Western Blot) -> SERS spectral analysis and ML implementation (PCA-SVM, CNN, RNN) -> Validation with clinical tear and sweat samples -> Molecular Dynamics simulation of binding mechanism.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

Figure 7: a. Workflow schematic depicting tear collection from patients with ocular diseases, SERS-based spectroscopic analysis, and subsequent machine learning-aided classification. b. Typical SERS spectra acquired from tear samples of patients with seven distinct ocular diseases and healthy volunteers. c-e.
